Quae gratia currum <sup>654</sup>armorumque fuit vivis, quae cura nitentis <sup>655</sup>pascere equos, eadem sequitur tellure repostos. <sup>656</sup>Conspicit, ecce, alios dextra laevaque per herbam <sup>657</sup>vescentis, laetumque choro paeana canentis <sup>658</sup>inter odoratum lauri nemus, unde superne <sup>659</sup>plurimus Eridani per silvam volvitur amnis. <sup>660</sup>Hic manus ob patriam pugnando volnera passi, <sup>661</sup>quique sacerdotes casti, dum vita manebat, <sup>662</sup>quique pii vates et Phoebo digna locuti, <sup>663</sup>inventas aut qui vitam excoluere per artes, <sup>664</sup>quique sui memores alios fecere merendo, <sup>665</sup>omnibus his nivea cinguntur tempora vitta. <sup>666</sup>Quos circumfusos sic est adfata Sybilla, <sup>667</sup>Musaeum ante omnes, medium nam plurima turba <sup>668</sup>hunc habet, atque umeris exstantem suspicit altis: <sup>669</sup>Dicite, felices animae, tuque, optime vates, <sup>670</sup>quae regio Anchisen, quis habet locus? Illius ergo <sup>671</sup>venimus, et magnos Erebi transnavimus amnes. <sup>672</sup>Atque huic responsum paucis ita reddidit heros: <sup>673</sup>Nulli certa domus; lucis habitamus opacis, <sup>674</sup>riparumque toros et prata recentia rivis <sup>675</sup>incolimus. Sed vos, si fert ita corde voluntas,

<sup>676</sup>hoc superate iugum; et facili iam tramite sistam. <sup>677</sup>Dixit, et ante tulit gressum, camposque nitentis <sup>678</sup>desuper ostentat; dehinc summa cacumina linquunt. <sup>679</sup>At pater Anchises penitus convalle virenti <sup>680</sup>inclusas animas superumque ad lumen ituras <sup>681</sup>lustrabat studio recolens, omnemque suorum <sup>682</sup>forte recensebat numerum carosque nepotes, <sup>683</sup>fataque fortunasque virum moresque manusque. <sup>684</sup>Isque ubi tendentem adversum per gramina videt <sup>685</sup>Aenean, alacris palmas utrasque tetendit, <sup>686</sup>effusaeque genis lacrimae, et vox excidit ore: <sup>687</sup>Venisti tandem, tuaque exspectata parenti <sup>688</sup>vicit iter durum pietas? Datur ora tueri, <sup>689</sup>nate, tua, et notas audire et reddere voces? <sup>690</sup>Sic equidem ducebam animo rebarque futurum, <sup>691</sup>tempora dinumerans, nec me mea cura fefellit. <sup>692</sup>Quas ego te terras et quanta per aequora vectum <sup>693</sup>accipio! quantis iactatum, nate, periclis! <sup>694</sup>Quam metui, ne quid Libyae tibi regna nocerent! <sup>695</sup>Ille autem: Tua me, genitor, tua tristis imago, <sup>696</sup>saepius occurrens, haec limina tendere adegit: <sup>697</sup>stant sale Tyrrheno classes. Da iungere dextram, <sup>698</sup>da, genitor, teque amplexu ne subtrahe nostro. <sup>699</sup>Sic memorans, largo fletu simul ora rigabat. <sup>700</sup>Ter conatus ibi collo dare brachia circum,
translation.text: She said, and thro’ the gloomy shades they pass’d, And chose the middle path. Arriv’d at last, The prince with living water sprinkled o’er His limbs and body; then approach’d the door, Possess’d the porch, and on the front above He fix’d the fatal bough requir’d by Pluto’s love. These holy rites perform’d, they took their way Where long extended plains of pleasure lay: The verdant fields with those of heav’n may vie, With ether vested, and a purple sky; The blissful seats of happy souls below. Stars of their own, and their own suns, they know; Their airy limbs in sports they exercise, And on the green contend the wrestler’s prize. Some in heroic verse divinely sing; Others in artful measures led the ring. The Thracian bard, surrounded by the rest, There stands conspicuous in his flowing vest; His flying fingers, and harmonious quill, Strikes sev’n distinguish’d notes, and sev’n at once they fill. Here found they Teucer’s old heroic race, Born better times and happier years to grace. Assaracus and Ilus here enjoy Perpetual fame, with him who founded Troy. The chief beheld their chariots from afar, Their shining arms, and coursers train’d to war: Their lances fix’d in earth, their steeds around, Free from their harness, graze the flow’ry ground. The love of horses which they had, alive, And care of chariots, after death survive. Some cheerful souls were feasting on the plain; Some did the song, and some the choir maintain, Beneath a laurel shade, where mighty Po Mounts up to woods above, and hides his head below. Here patriots live, who, for their country’s good, In fighting fields, were prodigal of blood: Priests of unblemish’d lives here make abode, And poets worthy their inspiring god; And searching wits, of more mechanic parts, Who grac’d their age with new-invented arts: Those who to worth their bounty did extend, And those who knew that bounty to commend. The heads of these with holy fillets bound, And all their temples were with garlands crown’d.

translation.original_text: <sup>701</sup>ter frustra comprensa manus effugit imago, <sup>702</sup>par levibus ventis volucrique simillima somno. <sup>703</sup>Interea videt Aeneas in valle reducta <sup>704</sup>seclusum nemus et virgulta sonantia silvis, <sup>705</sup>Lethaeumque, domos placidas qui praenatat, amnem. <sup>706</sup>Hunc circum innumerae gentes populique volabant; <sup>707</sup>ac—velut in pratis ubi apes aestate serena <sup>708</sup>floribus insidunt variis, et candida circum <sup>709</sup>lilia funduntur—strepit omnis murmure campus. <sup>710</sup>Horrescit visu subito, causasque requirit <sup>711</sup>inscius Aeneas, quae sint ea flumina porro, <sup>712</sup>quive viri tanto complerint agmine ripas. <sup>713</sup>Tum pater Anchises: Animae, quibus altera fato <sup>714</sup>corpora debentur, Lethaei ad fluminis undam <sup>715</sup>securos latices et longa oblivia potant. <sup>716</sup>Has equidem memorare tibi atque ostendere coram, <sup>717</sup>iampridem hanc prolem cupio enumerare meorum, <sup>718</sup>quo magis Italia mecum laetere reperta. <sup>719</sup>O pater, anne aliquas ad caelum hinc ire putandum est <sup>720</sup>sublimis animas, iterumque ad tarda reverti <sup>721</sup>corpora? Quae lucis miseris tam dira cupido? <sup>722</sup>Dicam equidem, nec te suspensum, nate, tenebo <sup>723</sup>suscipit Anchises, atque ordine singula pandit. <sup>724</sup>Principio caelum ac terras camposque liquentis <sup>725</sup>lucentemque globum Lunae Titaniaque astra
translation.text: To these the Sibyl thus her speech address’d, And first to him surrounded by the rest Tow’ring his height, and ample was his breast; “Say, happy souls, divine Musaeus, say, Where lives Anchises, and where lies our way To find the hero, for whose only sake We sought the dark abodes, and cross’d the bitter lake?” To this the sacred poet thus replied: “In no fix’d place the happy souls reside. In groves we live, and lie on mossy beds, By crystal streams, that murmur thro’ the meads: But pass yon easy hill, and thence descend; The path conducts you to your journey’s end.” This said, he led them up the mountain’s brow, And shews them all the shining fields below. They wind the hill, and thro’ the blissful meadows go.

translation.original_text: <sup>726</sup>spiritus intus alit, totamque infusa per artus <sup>727</sup>mens agitat molem et magno se corpore miscet. <sup>728</sup>Inde hominum pecudumque genus, vitaeque volantum, <sup>729</sup>et quae marmoreo fert monstra sub aequore pontus. <sup>730</sup>Igneus est ollis vigor et caelestis origo <sup>731</sup>seminibus, quantum non noxia corpora tardant, <sup>732</sup>terrenique hebetant artus moribundaque membra. <sup>733</sup>Hinc metuunt cupiuntque, dolent gaudentque, neque auras <sup>734</sup>dispiciunt clausae tenebris et carcere caeco. <sup>735</sup>Quin et supremo cum lumine vita reliquit, <sup>736</sup>non tamen omne malum miseris nec funditus omnes <sup>737</sup>corporeae excedunt pestes, penitusque necesse est <sup>738</sup>multa diu concreta modis inolescere miris. <sup>739</sup>Ergo exercentur poenis, veterumque malorum <sup>740</sup>supplicia expendunt: aliae panduntur inanes <sup>741</sup>suspensae ad ventos; aliis sub gurgite vasto <sup>742</sup>infectum eluitur scelus, aut exuritur igni; <sup>743</sup>quisque suos patimur Manes; exinde per amplum <sup>744</sup>mittimur Elysium, et pauci laeta arva tenemus; <sup>745</sup>donec longa dies, perfecto temporis orbe, <sup>746</sup>concretam exemit labem, purumque relinquit <sup>747</sup>aetherium sensum atque auraï simplicis ignem. <sup>748</sup>Has omnes, ubi mille rotam volvere per annos, <sup>749</sup>Lethaeum ad fluvium deus evocat agmine magno, <sup>750</sup>scilicet immemores supera ut convexa revisant,
translation.text: But old Anchises, in a flow’ry vale, Review’d his muster’d race, and took the tale: Those happy spirits, which, ordain’d by fate, For future beings and new bodies wait. With studious thought observ’d th’ illustrious throng, In nature’s order as they pass’d along: Their names, their fates, their conduct, and their care, In peaceful senates and successful war. He, when Aeneas on the plain appears, Meets him with open arms, and falling tears. “Welcome,” he said, “the gods’ undoubted race! O long expected to my dear embrace! Once more ’tis giv’n me to behold your face! The love and pious duty which you pay Have pass’d the perils of so hard a way. ’Tis true, computing times, I now believ’d The happy day approach’d; nor are my hopes deceiv’d. What length of lands, what oceans have you pass’d; What storms sustain’d, and on what shores been cast? How have I fear’d your fate! but fear’d it most, When love assail’d you, on the Libyan coast.” To this, the filial duty thus replies: “Your sacred ghost before my sleeping eyes Appear’d, and often urg’d this painful enterprise. After long tossing on the Tyrrhene sea, My navy rides at anchor in the bay. But reach your hand, O parent shade, nor shun The dear embraces of your longing son!” He said; and falling tears his face bedew: Then thrice around his neck his arms he threw; And thrice the flitting shadow slipp’d away, Like winds, or empty dreams that fly the day.

translation.original_text: <sup>751</sup>rursus et incipiant in corpora velle reverti. <sup>752</sup>Dixerat Anchises, natumque unaque Sibyllam <sup>753</sup>conventus trahit in medios turbamque sonantem, <sup>754</sup>et tumulum capit, unde omnes longo ordine possit <sup>755</sup>adversos legere, et venientum discere vultus. <sup>756</sup>Nunc age, Dardaniam prolem quae deinde sequatur <sup>757</sup>gloria, qui maneant Itala de gente nepotes, <sup>758</sup>inlustris animas nostrumque in nomen ituras, <sup>759</sup>expediam dictis, et te tua fata docebo. <sup>760</sup>Ille, vides, pura iuvenis qui nititur hasta, <sup>761</sup>proxuma sorte tenet lucis loca, primus ad auras <sup>762</sup>aetherias Italo commixtus sanguine surget, <sup>763</sup>silvius, Albanum nomen, tua postuma proles, <sup>764</sup>quem tibi longaevo serum Lavinia coniunx <sup>765</sup>educet silvis regem regumque parentem, <sup>766</sup>unde genus Longa nostrum dominabitur Alba. <sup>767</sup>Proxumus ille Procas, Troianae gloria gentis, <sup>768</sup>et Capys, et Numitor, et qui te nomine reddet <sup>769</sup>Silvius Aeneas, pariter pietate vel armis <sup>770</sup>egregius, si umquam regnandam acceperit Albam. <sup>771</sup>Qui iuvenes! Quantas ostentant, aspice, vires, <sup>772</sup>atque umbrata gerunt civili tempora quercu! <sup>773</sup>Hi tibi Nomentum et Gabios urbemque Fidenam, <sup>774</sup>hi Collatinas imponent montibus arces, <sup>775</sup>Pometios Castrumque Inui Bolamque Coramque.
translation.text: Now, in a secret vale, the Trojan sees A sep’rate grove, thro’ which a gentle breeze Plays with a passing breath, and whispers thro’ the trees; And, just before the confines of the wood, The gliding Lethe leads her silent flood. About the boughs an airy nation flew, Thick as the humming bees, that hunt the golden dew; In summer’s heat on tops of lilies feed, And creep within their bells, to suck the balmy seed: The winged army roams the fields around; The rivers and the rocks remurmur to the sound. Aeneas wond’ring stood, then ask’d the cause Which to the stream the crowding people draws. Then thus the sire: “The souls that throng the flood Are those to whom, by fate, are other bodies ow’d: In Lethe’s lake they long oblivion taste, Of future life secure, forgetful of the past. Long has my soul desir’d this time and place, To set before your sight your glorious race, That this presaging joy may fire your mind To seek the shores by destiny design’d.” “O father, can it be, that souls sublime Return to visit our terrestrial clime, And that the gen’rous mind, releas’d by death, Can covet lazy limbs and mortal breath?”

translation.original_text: <sup>776</sup>Haec tum nomina erunt, nunc sunt sine nomine terrae. <sup>777</sup>Quin et avo comitem sese Mavortius addet <sup>778</sup>Romulus, Assaraci quem sanguinis Ilia mater <sup>779</sup>educet. Viden, ut geminae stant vertice cristae, <sup>780</sup>et pater ipse suo superum iam signat honore? <sup>781</sup>En, huius, nate, auspiciis illa incluta Roma <sup>782</sup>imperium terris, animos aequabit Olympo, <sup>783</sup>septemque una sibi muro circumdabit arces, <sup>784</sup>felix prole virum: qualis Berecyntia mater <sup>785</sup>invehitur curru Phrygias turrita per urbes, <sup>786</sup>laeta deum partu, centum complexa nepotes, <sup>787</sup>omnes caelicolas, omnes supera alta tenentes. <sup>788</sup>Huc geminas nunc flecte acies, hanc aspice gentem <sup>789</sup>Romanosque tuos. Hic Caesar et omnis Iuli <sup>790</sup>progenies magnum caeli ventura sub axem. <sup>791</sup>Hic vir, hic est, tibi quem promitti saepius audis, <sup>792</sup>Augustus Caesar, Divi genus, aurea condet <sup>793</sup>saecula qui rursus Latio regnata per arva <sup>794</sup>Saturno quondam, super et Garamantas et Indos <sup>795</sup>proferet imperium: iacet extra sidera tellus, <sup>796</sup>extra anni solisque vias, ubi caelifer Atlas <sup>797</sup>axem umero torquet stellis ardentibus aptum. <sup>798</sup>Huius in adventum iam nunc et Caspia regna <sup>799</sup>responsis horrent divom et Maeotia tellus, <sup>800</sup>et septemgemini turbant trepida ostia Nili.
translation.text: Anchises then, in order, thus begun To clear those wonders to his godlike son: “Know, first, that heav’n, and earth’s compacted frame, And flowing waters, and the starry flame, And both the radiant lights, one common soul Inspires and feeds, and animates the whole. This active mind, infus’d thro’ all the space, Unites and mingles with the mighty mass. Hence men and beasts the breath of life obtain, And birds of air, and monsters of the main. Th’ ethereal vigour is in all the same, And every soul is fill’d with equal flame; As much as earthy limbs, and gross allay Of mortal members, subject to decay, Blunt not the beams of heav’n and edge of day. From this coarse mixture of terrestrial parts, Desire and fear by turns possess their hearts, And grief, and joy; nor can the groveling mind, In the dark dungeon of the limbs confin’d, Assert the native skies, or own its heav’nly kind: Nor death itself can wholly wash their stains; But long-contracted filth ev’n in the soul remains. The relics of inveterate vice they wear, And spots of sin obscene in ev’ry face appear. For this are various penances enjoin’d; And some are hung to bleach upon the wind, Some plung’d in waters, others purg’d in fires, Till all the dregs are drain’d, and all the rust expires. All have their manes, and those manes bear: The few, so cleans’d, to these abodes repair, And breathe, in ample fields, the soft Elysian air. Then are they happy, when by length of time The scurf is worn away of each committed crime; No speck is left of their habitual stains, But the pure ether of the soul remains. But, when a thousand rolling years are past, (So long their punishments and penance last,) Whole droves of minds are, by the driving god, Compell’d to drink the deep Lethaean flood, In large forgetful draughts to steep the cares Of their past labours, and their irksome years, That, unrememb’ring of its former pain, The soul may suffer mortal flesh again.”

translation.original_text: <sup>801</sup>Nec vero Alcides tantum telluris obivit, <sup>802</sup>fixerit aeripedem cervam licet, aut Erymanthi <sup>803</sup>pacarit nemora, et Lernam tremefecerit arcu; <sup>804</sup>nec, qui pampineis victor iuga flectit habenis, <sup>805</sup>Liber, agens celso Nysae de vertice tigres. <sup>806</sup>Et dubitamus adhuc virtute extendere vires, <sup>807</sup>aut metus Ausonia prohibet consistere terra? <sup>808</sup>Quis procul ille autem ramis insignis olivae <sup>809</sup>sacra ferens? Nosco crines incanaque menta <sup>810</sup>regis Romani, primus qui legibus urbem <sup>811</sup>fundabit, Curibus parvis et paupere terra <sup>812</sup>missus in imperium magnum. Cui deinde subibit, <sup>813</sup>otia qui rumpet patriae residesque movebit <sup>814</sup>Tullus in arma viros et iam desueta triumphis <sup>815</sup>agmina. Quem iuxta sequitur iactantior Ancus, <sup>816</sup>nunc quoque iam nimium gaudens popularibus auris. <sup>817</sup>Vis et Tarquinios reges, animamque superbam <sup>818</sup>ultoris Bruti, fascesque videre receptos? <sup>819</sup>Consulis imperium hic primus saevasque secures <sup>820</sup>accipiet, natosque pater nova bella moventes <sup>821</sup>ad poenam pulchra pro libertate vocabit. <sup>822</sup>Infelix, utcumque ferent ea facta minores, <sup>823</sup>vincet amor patriae laudumque immensa cupido. <sup>824</sup>Quin Decios Drusosque procul saevumque securi <sup>825</sup>aspice Torquatum et referentem signa Camillum.
translation.text: Thus having said, the father spirit leads The priestess and his son thro’ swarms of shades, And takes a rising ground, from thence to see The long procession of his progeny. “Survey,” pursued the sire, “this airy throng, As, offer’d to thy view, they pass along. These are th’ Italian names, which fate will join With ours, and graff upon the Trojan line. Observe the youth who first appears in sight, And holds the nearest station to the light, Already seems to snuff the vital air, And leans just forward, on a shining spear: Silvius is he, thy last-begotten race, But first in order sent, to fill thy place; An Alban name, but mix’d with Dardan blood, Born in the covert of a shady wood: Him fair Lavinia, thy surviving wife, Shall breed in groves, to lead a solitary life. In Alba he shall fix his royal seat, And, born a king, a race of kings beget. Then Procas, honour of the Trojan name, Capys, and Numitor, of endless fame. A second Silvius after these appears; Silvius Aeneas, for thy name he bears; For arms and justice equally renown’d, Who, late restor’d, in Alba shall be crown’d. How great they look! how vig’rously they wield Their weighty lances, and sustain the shield! But they, who crown’d with oaken wreaths appear, Shall Gabian walls and strong Fidena rear; Nomentum, Bola, with Pometia, found; And raise Collatian tow’rs on rocky ground. All these shall then be towns of mighty fame, Tho’ now they lie obscure, and lands without a name. See Romulus the great, born to restore The crown that once his injur’d grandsire wore. This prince a priestess of your blood shall bear, And like his sire in arms he shall appear. Two rising crests, his royal head adorn; Born from a god, himself to godhead born: His sire already signs him for the skies, And marks the seat amidst the deities. Auspicious chief! thy race, in times to come, Shall spread the conquests of imperial Rome. Rome, whose ascending tow’rs shall heav’n invade, Involving earth and ocean in her shade; High as the Mother of the Gods in place, And proud, like her, of an immortal race. Then, when in pomp she makes the Phrygian round, With golden turrets on her temples crown’d; A hundred gods her sweeping train supply; Her offspring all, and all command the sky.

translation.original_text: <sup>826</sup>Illae autem, paribus quas fulgere cernis in armis, <sup>827</sup>concordes animae nunc et dum nocte premuntur, <sup>828</sup>heu quantum inter se bellum, si lumina vitae <sup>829</sup>attigerint, quantas acies stragemque ciebunt! <sup>830</sup>Aggeribus socer Alpinis atque arce Monoeci <sup>831</sup>descendens, gener adversis instructus Eois. <sup>832</sup>Ne, pueri, ne tanta animis adsuescite bella, <sup>833</sup>neu patriae validas in viscera vertite vires; <sup>834</sup>tuque prior, tu parce, genus qui ducis Olympo, <sup>835</sup>proice tela manu, sanguis meus!— <sup>836</sup>Ille triumphata Capitolia ad alta Corintho <sup>837</sup>victor aget currum, caesis insignis Achivis. <sup>838</sup>Eruet ille Argos Agamemnoniasque Mycenas, <sup>839</sup>ipsumque Aeaciden, genus armipotentis Achilli, <sup>840</sup>ultus avos Troiae, templa et temerata Minervae. <sup>841</sup>Quis te, magne Cato, tacitum, aut te, Cosse, relinquat? <sup>842</sup>Quis Gracchi genus, aut geminos, duo fulmina belli, <sup>843</sup>Scipiadas, cladem Libyae, parvoque potentem <sup>844</sup>Fabricium vel te sulco Serrane, serentem? <sup>845</sup>quo fessum rapitis, Fabii? Tu Maxumus ille es, <sup>846</sup>unus qui nobis cunctando restituis rem. <sup>847</sup>Excudent alii spirantia mollius aera, <sup>848</sup>credo equidem, vivos ducent de marmore voltus, <sup>849</sup>orabunt causas melius, caelique meatus <sup>850</sup>describent radio, et surgentia sidera dicent:
translation.text: “Now fix your sight, and stand intent, to see Your Roman race, and Julian progeny. The mighty Caesar waits his vital hour, Impatient for the world, and grasps his promis’d pow’r. But next behold the youth of form divine, Caesar himself, exalted in his line; Augustus, promis’d oft, and long foretold, Sent to the realm that Saturn rul’d of old; Born to restore a better age of gold. Afric and India shall his pow’r obey; He shall extend his propagated sway Beyond the solar year, without the starry way, Where Atlas turns the rolling heav’ns around, And his broad shoulders with their lights are crown’d. At his foreseen approach, already quake The Caspian kingdoms and Maeotian lake: Their seers behold the tempest from afar, And threat’ning oracles denounce the war. Nile hears him knocking at his sev’nfold gates, And seeks his hidden spring, and fears his nephew’s fates. Nor Hercules more lands or labours knew, Not tho’ the brazen-footed hind he slew, Freed Erymanthus from the foaming boar, And dipp’d his arrows in Lernaean gore; Nor Bacchus, turning from his Indian war, By tigers drawn triumphant in his car, From Nisus’ top descending on the plains, With curling vines around his purple reins. And doubt we yet thro’ dangers to pursue The paths of honour, and a crown in view? But what’s the man, who from afar appears? His head with olive crown’d, his hand a censer bears, His hoary beard and holy vestments bring His lost idea back: I know the Roman king. He shall to peaceful Rome new laws ordain, Call’d from his mean abode a scepter to sustain. Him Tullus next in dignity succeeds, An active prince, and prone to martial deeds. He shall his troops for fighting fields prepare, Disus’d to toils, and triumphs of the war. By dint of sword his crown he shall increase, And scour his armour from the rust of peace. Whom Ancus follows, with a fawning air, But vain within, and proudly popular. Next view the Tarquin kings, th’ avenging sword Of Brutus, justly drawn, and Rome restor’d. He first renews the rods and ax severe, And gives the consuls royal robes to wear. His sons, who seek the tyrant to sustain, And long for arbitrary lords again, With ignominy scourg’d, in open sight, He dooms to death deserv’d, asserting public right. Unhappy man, to break the pious laws Of nature, pleading in his children’s cause! Howe’er the doubtful fact is understood, ’Tis love of honour, and his country’s good: The consul, not the father, sheds the blood. Behold Torquatus the same track pursue; And, next, the two devoted Decii view: The Drusian line, Camillus loaded home With standards well redeem’d, and foreign foes o’ercome The pair you see in equal armour shine, Now, friends below, in close embraces join; But, when they leave the shady realms of night, And, cloth’d in bodies, breathe your upper light, With mortal hate each other shall pursue: What wars, what wounds, what slaughter shall ensue! From Alpine heights the father first descends; His daughter’s husband in the plain attends: His daughter’s husband arms his eastern friends. Embrace again, my sons, be foes no more; Nor stain your country with her children’s gore! And thou, the first, lay down thy lawless claim, Thou, of my blood, who bear’st the Julian name! Another comes, who shall in triumph ride, And to the Capitol his chariot guide, From conquer’d Corinth, rich with Grecian spoils. And yet another, fam’d for warlike toils, On Argos shall impose the Roman laws, And on the Greeks revenge the Trojan cause; Shall drag in chains their Achillean race; Shall vindicate his ancestors’ disgrace, And Pallas, for her violated place. Great Cato there, for gravity renown’d, And conqu’ring Cossus goes with laurels crown’d. Who can omit the Gracchi? who declare The Scipios’ worth, those thunderbolts of war, The double bane of Carthage? Who can see Without esteem for virtuous poverty, Severe Fabricius, or can cease t’ admire The plowman consul in his coarse attire? Tir’d as I am, my praise the Fabii claim; And thou, great hero, greatest of thy name, Ordain’d in war to save the sinking state, And, by delays, to put a stop to fate! Let others better mould the running mass Of metals, and inform the breathing brass, And soften into flesh a marble face; Plead better at the bar; describe the skies, And when the stars descend, and when they rise. But, Rome, ’tis thine alone, with awful sway, To rule mankind, and make the world obey, Disposing peace and war by thy own majestic way; To tame the proud, the fetter’d slave to free: These are imperial arts, and worthy thee.”

translation.original_text: <sup>851</sup>tu regere imperio populos, Romane, memento; <sup>852</sup>hae tibi erunt artes; pacisque imponere morem, <sup>853</sup>parcere subiectis, et debellare superbos. <sup>854</sup>Sic pater Anchises, atque haec mirantibus addit: <sup>855</sup>Aspice, ut insignis spoliis Marcellus opimis <sup>856</sup>ingreditur, victorque viros supereminet omnes! <sup>857</sup>Hic rem Romanam, magno turbante tumultu, <sup>858</sup>sistet, eques sternet Poenos Gallumque rebellem, <sup>859</sup>tertiaque arma patri suspendet capta Quirino. <sup>860</sup>Atque hic Aeneas; una namque ire videbat <sup>861</sup>egregium forma iuvenem et fulgentibus armis, <sup>862</sup>sed frons laeta parum, et deiecto lumina voltu: <sup>863</sup>Quis, pater, ille, virum qui sic comitatur euntem? <sup>864</sup>Filius, anne aliquis magna de stirpe nepotum? <sup>865</sup>Quis strepitus circa comitum! Quantum instar in ipso! <sup>866</sup>Sed nox atra caput tristi circumvolat umbra. <sup>867</sup>Tum pater Anchises, lacrimis ingressus obortis: <sup>868</sup>O gnate, ingentem luctum ne quaere tuorum; <sup>869</sup>ostendent terris hunc tantum fata, neque ultra <sup>870</sup>esse sinent. Nimium vobis Romana propago <sup>871</sup>visa potens, Superi, propria haec si dona fuissent. <sup>872</sup>Quantos ille virum magnam Mavortis ad urbem <sup>873</sup>campus aget gemitus, vel quae, Tiberine, videbis <sup>874</sup>funera, cum tumulum praeterlabere recentem! <sup>875</sup>Nec puer Iliaca quisquam de gente Latinos
translation.text: He paus’d; and, while with wond’ring eyes they view’d The passing spirits, thus his speech renew’d: “See great Marcellus! how, untir’d in toils, He moves with manly grace, how rich with regal spoils! He, when his country, threaten’d with alarms, Requires his courage and his conqu’ring arms, Shall more than once the Punic bands affright; Shall kill the Gaulish king in single fight; Then to the Capitol in triumph move, And the third spoils shall grace Feretrian Jove.” Aeneas here beheld, of form divine, A godlike youth in glitt’ring armour shine, With great Marcellus keeping equal pace; But gloomy were his eyes, dejected was his face. He saw, and, wond’ring, ask’d his airy guide, What and of whence was he, who press’d the hero’s side: “His son, or one of his illustrious name? How like the former, and almost the same! Observe the crowds that compass him around; All gaze, and all admire, and raise a shouting sound: But hov’ring mists around his brows are spread, And night, with sable shades, involves his head.” “Seek not to know,” the ghost replied with tears, “The sorrows of thy sons in future years. This youth (the blissful vision of a day) Shall just be shown on earth, and snatch’d away. The gods too high had rais’d the Roman state, Were but their gifts as permanent as great. What groans of men shall fill the Martian field! How fierce a blaze his flaming pile shall yield! What fun’ral pomp shall floating Tiber see, When, rising from his bed, he views the sad solemnity! No youth shall equal hopes of glory give, No youth afford so great a cause to grieve; The Trojan honour, and the Roman boast, Admir’d when living, and ador’d when lost! Mirror of ancient faith in early youth! Undaunted worth, inviolable truth! No foe, unpunish’d, in the fighting field Shall dare thee, foot to foot, with sword and shield; Much less in arms oppose thy matchless force, When thy sharp spurs shall urge thy foaming horse. Ah! couldst thou break thro’ fate’s severe decree, A new Marcellus shall arise in thee! Full canisters of fragrant lilies bring, Mix’d with the purple roses of the spring; Let me with fun’ral flow’rs his body strow; This gift which parents to their children owe, This unavailing gift, at least, I may bestow!” Thus having said, he led the hero round The confines of the blest Elysian ground; Which when Anchises to his son had shown, And fir’d his mind to mount the promis’d throne, He tells the future wars, ordain’d by fate; The strength and customs of the Latian state; The prince, and people; and forearms his care With rules, to push his fortune, or to bear.

translation.original_text: <sup>876</sup>in tantum spe tollet avos, nec Romula quondam <sup>877</sup>ullo se tantum tellus iactabit alumno. <sup>878</sup>Heu pietas, heu prisca fides, invictaque bello <sup>879</sup>dextera! Non illi se quisquam impune tulisset <sup>880</sup>obvius armato, seu cum pedes iret in hostem, <sup>881</sup>seu spumantis equi foderet calcaribus armos. <sup>882</sup>Heu, miserande puer, si qua fata aspera rumpas, <sup>883</sup>tu Marcellus eris. Manibus date lilia plenis, <sup>884</sup>purpureos spargam flores, animamque nepotis <sup>885</sup>his saltem adcumulem donis, et fungar inani <sup>886</sup>munere—Sic tota passim regione vagantur <sup>887</sup>aëris in campis latis, atque omnia lustrant. <sup>888</sup>Quae postquam Anchises natum per singula duxit, <sup>889</sup>incenditque animum famae venientis amore, <sup>890</sup>exin bella viro memorat quae deinde gerenda, <sup>891</sup>Laurentisque docet populos urbemque Latini, <sup>892</sup>et quo quemque modo fugiatque feratque laborem. <sup>893</sup>Sunt geminae Somni portae, quarum altera fertur <sup>894</sup>cornea, qua veris facilis datur exitus umbris; <sup>895</sup>altera candenti perfecta nitens elephanto, <sup>896</sup>sed falsa ad caelum mittunt insomnia Manes. <sup>897</sup>His ubi tum natum Anchises unaque Sibyllam <sup>898</sup>prosequitur dictis, portaque emittit eburna, <sup>899</sup>ille viam secat ad naves sociosque revisit: <sup>900</sup>tum se ad Caietae recto fert litore portum.
<sup>901</sup>Ancora de prora iacitur, stant litore puppes.
translation.text: Two gates the silent house of Sleep adorn; Of polish’d ivory this, that of transparent horn: True visions thro’ transparent horn arise; Thro’ polish’d ivory pass deluding lies. Of various things discoursing as he pass’d, Anchises hither bends his steps at last. Then, thro’ the gate of iv’ry, he dismiss’d His valiant offspring and divining guest. Straight to the ships Aeneas took his way, Embark’d his men, and skimm’d along the sea, Still coasting, till he gain’d Cajeta’s bay. At length on oozy ground his galleys moor; Their heads are turn’d to sea, their sterns to shore.
